speaker: interviewer
text: February 25, 1997. The interviewer is Nina Elazar-Wolff. The interview takes place in Bat Yam, Israel in the English language. At the last cassette, we were saying about Vaboulis, who got a sentence of 10 years and that you managed to see him two minutes. Please go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, a [NON-ENGLISH] came to my house. I had a fractured arm. His name was [NON-ENGLISH], a Jew, a captain of the KGB. He took-- he and another man took me to the KGB. On my way-- we walked to the KGB-- on my way, he asked me, do you know old Hebrew? Yes. Do you believe in God? Yes. I decided not to humiliate-- not to give him any chance to humiliate myself, not to, not to say that I am a holy communist, because-- I didn't want to do that. And I did-- I, I knew for sure that they would never release me. I was aware I didn't know for what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the KGB-- the [NON-ENGLISH] had given his consent for me to be arrested. What did they accuse you for?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] activities. But I have omitted here a piece of Kovno. I have omitted here. Just a minute. Before, before this happened-- just a minute. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yes, before-- how, how-- now I am confused about everything. I, Grishe Hait, when I met him, found me in the house of a Baptist family, where I was hiding. And we understood that these people couldn't hide me for a very long time. They had lots of small children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you need hiding for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that the KGB would arrest me. That was clear already. So just getting away. My friends mentioned to my nanny that I was-- I had gone to Sweden. They wanted to confuse her, because she was an older person and in case KGB starts investigating, she would tell them that I'm in Sweden. They wouldn't be able to, to find me there. And that was all wrong. So Grishe knew where I was hiding, and he suggested I go to Kovno for a month, where he had an uncle, a dermatologist, with his wife. They were very sorry for me. And they took me in a truck to Kovno. There, I was hiding a whole month. In that house, I got forged papers from Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], who is a man who was living here, for very little money.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was [INAUDIBLE]. And I traveled to Novosibirsk or not so far from Novosibirsk. In Cherepanovo, my brother was living with his wife and child. And I decided to hide there from the KGB.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the same, in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which months?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was winter already-- November, I believe. There was a lot of snow there, and I couldn't go out. I didn't want people to see me. I didn't want my brother to have any problems. So I was sitting, and I was translating a Russian book into several languages-- into too many languages. That was also a disaster. Suddenly, the door opened, and a colonel of the KGB entered into the house with another KGB man. And then they said, you are arrested. And then he searched the house, and they saw that I am translating into many languages, although they knew that I know several languages. And here I forgot to mention again that I had stolen a small bottle of a liquid. The label said it is poison. Unfortunately, it was not poison. It was opium. Because I thought at any moment, they're coming and finding me and coming to arrest me. I'm putting an end to my life. I asked to go to the toilet. The colonel-- against the Russian law, the colonel had come to arrest me without a woman. And he said to me, I see it on your face that you have done something in the toilet. And I can get a 10 years imprisonment if they find out that I have come without a woman. Please tell me what you have drunk. I didn't tell him, because I didn't know what I had drunk. But he phoned or asked the lady, who's a doctor, my brother's sister-in-law and, obviously, she told him. So and since the colonel's father was a doctor, he told him to give me a lot of milk to drink, which I did, which I was forced to do. And there, the investigation lasted, I believe, only, only that night, and then they were, they were taking me to a truck with a heap of coal to the center of Novosibirsk where there is the infamous [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] KGB prison of Novosibirsk. It's one of the worst prisons in the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your brother and his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Absolutely nothing. Because they knew that he was not guilty. My brother didn't send me a piece of bread. He didn't want to endanger his freedom and his family's freedom. And I was alone in the prison without any food. I was hungry all the time, whereas all the rest of the arrested women, prisoners, got food. Well, so I had thought-- I thought they had arrested my brother, but that was not true.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing there, your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was a main bookkeeper of the-- he was a main bookkeeper, and his wife was a main bookkeeper. They made a very good living there, financially. So he was well off.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your nanny?

speaker: interviewee
text: My nanny was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned Sweden.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my nanny, she mentioned Sweden. It happened-- the KGB didn't know where I was. They took me for a very dangerous and very unpredictable person. Perhaps they were right. [LAUGHS] So they knocked on-- one night, they knocked on my nanny's-- that's her story-- they knocked on her door in my house. It was in my house. And they said, where is Zlemah? And she was sleepy, and she said, oh, go and look for her in Sweden in her brother's house. Because she knew I'm in Sweden. She was told I'm in Sweden. My brother lives in [NON-ENGLISH], somewhere far away, so let them take the trouble and go to Novosibirsk to look for me. She wanted to harm them, to punish them. But she punished me, unfortunately. So they did. So the KGB, obviously, was in touch with the Novosibirsk KGB, and they gave the order to arrest me. That's all. That's how I was found.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you try and describe the conditions in Novosibirsk jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can, I will never forget them. I have been to many prisons in Russia, but that was the most terrible, unbelievable place. I showed you a small sack with the comb. That is a place where I was. My ward mates were-- my prison mates, my cell mates-- were Kalmyk women, all intelligent, all with university education, all innocent. That was a time in Russia where they arrested all these Kalmyk people. They're very nice to me and very-- tried to be very helpful. And I couldn't speak Russian, so it was really hard for me to communicate with them. So this small sack was given by one of the Kalmyk women, because she knew that, that I had such a lion's mane and that I had no comb or nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the cell was very long, dark, of course. As in all Russian cells, there is a small barred window only, near the ceiling. Don't approach the window. You will be shot. That's what they usually said. And rats-- not mice-- rats were running in the daytime in that cell and we got our [NON-ENGLISH], our so-called portion of black bread, which was on a small shelf. And we saw-- we watched the rats eating our bread, and we were afraid to kill them or to throw something at them. They were like, like wild animals, you know? I've never heard of that before. So there, I was about six weeks, I believe. What about investigations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nocturnal investigations every night. No sleeping in the daytime. Investigations, which were absolutely senseless, without any foundation. I didn't know what they wanted from me. I didn't know what they want me to say. They gave me sheets of papers and asked me to write-- all KGBs, the same, just write.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what language?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever language I wanted because they have got their interpreters. I even don't remember in what language I wrote. I believe in German. Well, never mind, and then I was sent to Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that prison, six weeks. But in the meantime, I was sent to Riga. It took them about seven weeks, I think, to get me to Riga. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If you have seen the trains to Auschwitz, I think-- and I can draw a parallel-- that the Russian trains are more horrible. We were like sardines. There was no place, no room to stand and no place to sit. Hungry, thirsty-- thirst, thirst is the worst thing a person can experience. You are ready to kill people. They give you salty fish to eat-- smoked, salted fish, very bad quality, and they don't give you the water. I saw people going mad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So until I-- when I arrived at Riga, at the Riga Central Prison, it seemed to me I'm in paradise in comparison. My dress was full of lice. My hair, my hair was full of other lice, special lice. So then there came the disinfection, yeah, where we were standing naked in front of men, men, taken to the baths in front of men. Standing-- you know, young girls, naked-- and they're standing and grinning at you, worse than the Germans. Well, this is all I experienced there. And then there came different, different interrogations again, whereby they wanted me to accuse other people, other people. They wanted me to mention names of other people who could also be accused or sent to prison for something. And those people were innocent, and I refused to do so. And at night, one night, I, I counted that about 16 or 17 people would have to go prison if I told them only the names of those people who have helped me escape and who have hidden me, you see? The Baptists, et cetera, et cetera-- there were very many people who have helped me. So I refused. And this is why they sent me to the lunatic asylum as a terrible punishment-- only this. There was no other reason for that. So you were in the Riga prison--

speaker: interviewee
text: Central Prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of the KGB?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The KGB, I was at the beginning. People are first in the KGB for interrogation. Then they are sent to prison. And occasionally they are taken from the prison to the KGB headquarters for interrogations. But they sleep and stay-- so to stay in, in the prison, that was Riga Central Prison, an old, old prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did ever somebody from the KGB on the interrogation, the Russian interrogation, ask you about your war experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never, ever. Never, ever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know what happened to you in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they knew everything, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: From you, I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: From me, they knew because I had to write that in, in Novosibirsk already, so the same story I am telling you, the whole, true story. Absolutely. But that was not good enough for them. They wanted to make of me an English American spy. And they didn't succeed, and they were terribly angry for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Through the whole time, did you have any contact with the outside, with your friends, family, nanny?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my nanny sometimes sent me a food parcel now and again from the countryside. She did. [NON-ENGLISH] mother and sister sometimes sent me a food parcel-- not my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what happened to Vaboulis in the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. I was, I was, I was free then when he was sentenced to 10 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but while you were in prison, I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was in the Central Prison of Riga and he was not sent to Russia yet, there was a [NON ENGLISH], a gathering point. So he found there a nurse who worked as a nurse also in the prison where I was sitting. And he found out through this Latvia nurse that I am alive and that I am in prison. And he sent me, on one occasion, an egg, I believe. It was illegal. And he made this-- I have it at home, a spoon made with his own hands, a spoon made of some cheap metal, et cetera. He was greatly worried about me, not about himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So till when were you in this prison in Riga?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here we have it-- on the 25th of September or October when it was-- when I was sent to the lunatic asylum. The first time I was let out free and lived with Grishe and gave birth to the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when, when was it when you came out of the prison in Riga?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you now. Count back about the beginning of the year-- the beginning of 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the beginning of 1948, you come back to Riga and you meet--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in Riga. The Central Prison is in Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean home and you meet Grishe.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I have no home anymore. My home is confiscated. We live in a-- [SIGHS] in one room in a collective in a mutual house, which Grishe's friend, a doctor, very nice person, has given it to us where to stay. And there, I find out that they're waiting until give birth to the baby, and then they're coming to arrest me. The [NON-ENGLISH] told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a minute. So you come-- you go out of prison. You meet Grishe. Do you marry him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we married in Novosibirsk, but it was on forged documents. Formally--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you lived together?

speaker: interviewee
text: --I am his wife, but it has been annulled. Our, our marriage was not legal. I am not Zlemah Hait anymore. I am Zlemah Shebshelovitz again.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you come out of the prison, are you still Mrs. Vaboulis?

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, I am. Even when my child was born, she was born as a child of Vaboulis, although Vaboulis was far away, because my marriage with Grishe was illegal in Novosibirsk-- illegal forged documents. And this is-- [INAUDIBLE]-- this is why they said this can't, it can't function this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you live with Grishe, and you get pregnant--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and give birth to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Give birth to a child, to Shulamit.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Shulamit. When?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19, September '48. And in five weeks' time, two KGB men, again, come and pick me up with my child and take me to the mad house, where I'm lying on-- there are special rooms in the mad house for prisoners, barred windows, et cetera-- on an iron cot, very small, very-- a [? metal ?] cot where I spend with her half a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you breastfeed her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. But when my child was taken away unexpectedly, I got a very high temperature and then mastitis, and I was very ill, but nobody cared.

speaker: interviewer
text: On what ground did they put you into the psychiatric hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no grounds given. They said that I am mad because I didn't give them the information they wanted. If I had told him the truth-- who had helped me, who had hidden me-- perhaps they would have released me. I don't, I don't know. Perhaps not. I can't tell you. You can't tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe the, the, the life, if I can say so, in the psychiatric asylum?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can. At the beginning, when I was with my child, the circumstances were more or less bearable, but I knew at any day, they can take the child away in the first place. It was like in prison. There was no difference between the mad house and prison. But after they had taken my child away, they took me to another building, to another department, which was a calm department at the beginning, a calm department. You could even conduct a conversation there with someone if you were lucky. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I found out the circumstances in my house [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at home were terrible, that there was no hope that I could cope with circumstances even if I came out. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I decided to commit suicide and very seriously. So I saved 36 Ruminol and Veronal tablets-- here, you know, in these cushions in my dressing gown. And one night, I went into the bathroom and, again, I came with [INAUDIBLE] I had a small, small photo hidden of my baby. And I looked at the photo and kissed the baby. And I went back to bed and later, there was a blackout. I lost consciousness. And the lady who was lying next to me, very intelligent person from Leningrad, she noticed that my lips were blue and that I was unconscious. It was nighttime, and she called Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], the, the head doctor, who was very, very fond of me. And she came and she organized so that I was, that I came to. My stomach was pumped out and I was a day and a half unconscious. When I woke up, I found myself in the same bed, but I was taken to another department, which was in the same building, for chronically sick schizophrenics. And that was probably the worst time in my life, that nobody was a human being there. They were like animals-- jumping like tigers, like lions, beating me because they saw in my face someone ill. [INAUDIBLE] had hallucinations. I was beaten up all the time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But the KGB was informed. The director of the hospital had to inform the KGB that I had committed suicide and this was a punishment. I don't remember exactly how long I was there, but it was a long time. And later the Latvian doctors helped me to return to the quiet department, which was somehow bearable, but hopeless. And one night, Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] came in. She was on duty. And she said, your fate is being decided tomorrow morning. We have so arranged that a, a famous professor from Moscow, who has come to Riga to decide whether a soldier who was drinking cat's blood is normal or not, whether he should undergo punishment or not. So he will see you, and he will decide whether you are to stay here or to be sentenced to Serbsky Institute, as it had been decided before. And I knew Serbsky Institute is the end, terrible end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where  is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Moscow. Professor-- this General [NON-ENGLISH] and some more people were sent there, but people don't come out normal, even if they are released from there. Well, here in the hospital, I got thousands-- and in the Central Prison-- thousands of injections. I never knew what injections. They're making experiments, experiments on people. And he's Professor Timofejev, from Moscow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell the name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: T-I-M-O-F-E-J-E-V, Timofejev, wonderful person. So a Jewish doctor was reading-- a female doctor-- was reading my story, my [? tick, ?] and she said is that all? And he asked me, do you want to go back to your baby? I said, I don't care. I was in such a terrible depression [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I believe I said what I really meant. I was exhausted. I was unable to live any more, and I knew what's going on in my house. And I was released that same day. So the Latvian doctors helped me get home, et cetera, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it when you were released? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: March, I believe, 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they took the baby away, what happened to Shulamit? Where did she go?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was taken-- Grishe took her home. And my nanny was staying with him. And the nanny brought her up in a very loving, caring way, like as she brought me up, the same. She loved her, again, as her own child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she moved into Grishe's place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And the child was born. The child was born, and I was in the madhouse. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you see the child while you were in the madhouse?

speaker: interviewee
text: On very few occasions and illegally. So she smuggled her in thanks to, again, to some Latvian nurses, who were sorry for me, who helped me a lot. And I saw the baby for a couple of minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came back home, did the child recognize you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The child took me to the wall, where my picture was hanging, my portrait, and she said, auntie, look, that's my mommy. Mm hmm. She was-- no, she couldn't. She didn't know even the difference, probably, what her mommy and what an auntie is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe how you felt in this meeting.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's impossible. That is-- that's beyond my strengths. I very often recall it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, we had lost all our so-called friends. Nobody came to our house, because I was a political prisoner or had been a political prisoner. And this lasted for years and years, living without any documents, without anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if, if I can ask you, if you compare this period between '45 and '50-- five years of the KGB prisons under the Soviet Union and the German occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: My memories are, I'm afraid to say, more bitter of the Russian occupation than of the German occupation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First of all, I was younger. A couple of years don't make a difference, but I had already survived something terrible. And here, here, I saw that I couldn't survive, that there was no way to survive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Whenever I watch television about the Holocaust, I compare it, and I think that those railways and those cells and this attitude was even worse than the German. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, I met also there, nice people, lovely, helping, helpful people. But that's not a rule. That is the exception.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your brother all these years when you were in prison? Where was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived happily.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Moscow. Yes, came with his wife. He remarried, and he lived in Moscow. And when he knew that Stalin had died, started again coming to my house and living in our [NON ENGLISH], which we had rented every summer. And he was a very nice and friendly brother. And now he writes me letters from Chicago in Yiddish and that, only my letters, dear sister, keep him alive. And I do my very best. I answer him regularly.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's nice. And since March '50, what is your lifestyle? What are you doing in Riga?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Riga?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were living in Riga?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. 1950, there was no lifestyle at all. I didn't go out almost. I didn't go out. I had no passport, no ID card, nothing. I had to go twice a week to the police station to report that I'm there. And Grishe was responsible for my way of life, that I wouldn't commit any crime-- social crime-- as far as the Soviet government is concerned. It was a long time. And then periodically, they summoned me to the KGB, and they asked me different questions and they, then finally, they offered me a wonderful job and to provide my family with these wonderful things if I go to Sweden or somewhere else-- since I am a woman who knows many languages-- and work for them. Cynical approach. And thankfully I-- thankfully, I refused, thankfully.

speaker: interviewer
text: We finished this cassette now and we go on with the story in the next--
